devanagari: अथ षष्ठोऽध्यायः । सूत उवाच । एतन्निशम्य मुनिनाभिहितं परीक्षिद् । व्यासात्मजेन निखिलात्मदृशा समेन । तत्पादमूलमुपसृत्य नतेन मूर्ध्ना । बद्...
english_devnagari: atha ṣaṣṭho'dhyāyaḥsūta uvācaetanniśamya muninābhihitaṃ parīkṣidvyāsātmajena nikhilātmadṛśā samenatatpādamūlamupasṛtya natena mūrdhnābaddhāñjalistamidamāha sa viṣṇurātaḥ
translation:
Śūta said:
Having heard this exposition from the Sage Śuka, the son of Vyāsa, who had the vision of Lord Hari, the soul abiding in all[1] King Parīkṣit, who (while in womb) was protected by Lord Viṣṇu (Kṛṣṇa) approached the lotus-feet of Śuka and. bowed down with his head bent and palms folded.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Or: Who looked upon all as within himself or with equal outlook (Bhāvāratha Dīpikā).

devanagari: राजोवाच । सिद्धोऽस्म्यनुगृहीतोऽस्मि भवता करुणात्मना । श्रावितो यच्च मे साक्षादनादिनिधनो हरिः ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: rājovācasiddho'smyanugṛhīto'smi bhavatā karuṇātmanāśrāvito yacca me sākṣādanādinidhano hariḥ
translation:
The king Parīkṣit requested:
My purpose in life (viz, of gaining spiritual wisdom) has been accomplished. I have been graciously favoured by your worship of merciful heart, as I have been made to listen directly (to the stories etc.) of Lord Hari who is the cause of the world[2] and have been enlightened in the means of attaining to him.
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Or: Who is eternal with no beginning or end.

devanagari: नात्यद्भुतमहं मन्ये महतामच्युतात्मनाम् । अज्ञेषु तापतप्तेषु भूतेषु यदनुग्रहः ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: nātyadbhutamahaṃ manye mahatāmacyutātmanāmajñeṣu tāpatapteṣu bhūteṣu yadanugrahaḥ
translation: I do not consider it a wonder that the grace of those noble persons who have completely devoted their hearts to Acyuta (Kṛṣṇa) should be showered on ignorant beings who have been tormented by miseries.

devanagari: पुराणसंहितामेतामश्रौष्म भवतो वयम् । यस्यां खलूत्तमःश्लोको भगवाननवर्ण्यते ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: purāṇasaṃhitāmetāmaśrauṣma bhavato vayamyasyāṃ khalūttamaḥśloko bhagavānanavarṇyate
translation: We have heard from you the collection of this (Bhāgavata) Purāṇa in which is extolled and praised Lord Viṣṇu of hallowing renown.

devanagari: भगवंस्तक्षकादिभ्यो मृत्युभ्यो न बिभेम्यहम् । प्रविष्टो ब्रह्म निर्वाणमभयं दर्शितं त्वया ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: bhagavaṃstakṣakādibhyo mṛtyubhyo na bibhemyahampraviṣṭo brahma nirvāṇamabhayaṃ darśitaṃ tvayā
translation: Worshipful Sir! Now I am not afraid of death either from Takṣaka or from other causes. I have entered (i.e. established my mind) in the fearless and supremely blissful Brahman, as guided by you.

devanagari: अनुजानीहि मां ब्रह्मन्वाचं यच्छाम्यधोक्षजे । मुक्तकामाशयं चेतः प्रवेश्य विसृजाम्यसून् ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: anujānīhi māṃ brahmanvācaṃ yacchāmyadhokṣajemuktakāmāśayaṃ cetaḥ praveśya visṛjāmyasūn
translation: O Brāhmaṇa sage! Be pleased to permit me. I shall now control my speech and other senses and concentrate on the supra-sensuous Lord. Entering (i.e. establishing my mind which is rid of all cravings and desires into the Supreme Lord, I shall give up my body.

devanagari: अज्ञानं च निरस्तं मे ज्ञानविज्ञाननिष्ठया । भवता दर्शितं क्षेमं परं भगवतः पदम् ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: ajñānaṃ ca nirastaṃ me jñānavijñānaniṣṭhayābhavatā darśitaṃ kṣemaṃ paraṃ bhagavataḥ padam
translation: You have graciously shown to me the fearless and the supreme state of the Almighty Lord, the firm insight of the knowledge and realisation of which have completely wiped out my nescience and the effects and impressions caused by it.

devanagari: सूत उवाच । इत्युक्तस्तमनुज्ञाप्य भगवान्बादरायणिः । जगाम भिक्षुभिः साकं नरदेवेन पूजितः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: sūta uvācaityuktastamanujñāpya bhagavānbādarāyaṇiḥjagāma bhikṣubhiḥ sākaṃ naradevena pūjitaḥ
translation:
Sūta said:
The worshipful Śuka, the son of Bāḍarāyaṇa who was thus addressed and worshipped by the king, took leave of the king and departed along with other ascetics (who came to listen to the Bhāgavata).

devanagari: परीक्षिदपि राजर्षिरात्मन्यात्मानमात्मना । समाधाय परं दध्यावस्पन्दासुर्यथा तरुः ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: parīkṣidapi rājarṣirātmanyātmānamātmanāsamādhāya paraṃ dadhyāvaspandāsuryathā taruḥ
translation: By force of his reason, king Parīkṣit also established his mind firmly with his Soul and meditated on the Supreme Brahman remaining breathless and motionless like a tree (trunk or a post.)

devanagari: प्राक्कूले बर्हिष्यासीनो गङ्गाकूल उदङ्मुखः । ब्रह्मभूतो महायोगी निःसङ्गश्छिन्नसंशयः ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: prākkūle barhiṣyāsīno gaṅgākūla udaṅmukhaḥbrahmabhūto mahāyogī niḥsaṅgaśchinnasaṃśayaḥ
translation: On the bank of the Gaṅgā, facing to the north and seated on a seat of Kuśa grass the end of which pointed to the east, the great Yogin who was completely devoid of attachment and all whose doubts had been resolved, was completely merged in the Brahman.

devanagari: तक्षकः प्रहितो विप्राः क्रुद्धेन द्विजसूनुना । हन्तुकामो नृपं गच्छन्ददर्श पथि कश्यपम् ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: takṣakaḥ prahito viprāḥ kruddhena dvijasūnunāhantukāmo nṛpaṃ gacchandadarśa pathi kaśyapam
translation: O Brāhmaṇas! when the king became absorbed in Brahman, it was unnecessary and superfluous to kill him by snake-bite. In the meanwhile Takṣaka was directed by the revengeful and indignant son of Brāhmaṇa (sage Śamīka). While Takṣaka was going with the intention of killing the King, on the way he met (the Brāhmaṇa) Kaśyapa (who knew how to counteract poison).

devanagari: तं तर्पयित्वा द्रविणैर्निवर्त्य विषहारिणम् । द्विजरूपप्रतिच्छन्नः कामरूपोऽदशन्नृपम् ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ tarpayitvā draviṇairnivartya viṣahāriṇamdvijarūpapraticchannaḥ kāmarūpo'daśannṛpam
translation: With payment of money, he gratified the Brāhmaṇa who knew how to cure (all types of) poison[3] and made him go back. The serpent who was capable of assuming any form at will, disguised himself as a Brāhmaṇa[4] and bit the King.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
Mahābhārata: Ādi. 42.36-43 describes how Kāśyapa showed Takṣaka how he resuscitated the tree burnt to ashes by Takṣaka-bite.

Footnote [4]:
The Mahābhārata Ādi. 43. 22-36 makes him mixed up as a worm in the fruits, presented to the King by Nāga sages.

devanagari: ब्रह्मभूतस्य राजर्षेर्देहोऽहिगरलाग्निना । बभूव भस्मसात्सद्यः पश्यतां सर्वदेहिनाम् ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: brahmabhūtasya rājarṣerdeho'higaralāgninābabhūva bhasmasātsadyaḥ paśyatāṃ sarvadehinām
translation: Instantly the body of the royal sage who had become merged in Brahman was reduced to ashes by the serpent poison, while all embodied beings stood as mere witnesses.

devanagari: हाहाकारो महानासीद्भुवि खे दिक्षु सर्वतः । विस्मिता ह्यभवन्सर्वे देवासुरनरादयः ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: hāhākāro mahānāsīdbhuvi khe dikṣu sarvataḥvismitā hyabhavansarve devāsuranarādayaḥ
translation: There arose a loud hue and cry all over the earth, the sky and in all directions. For all gods, asuras, men, etc. became astonished at the event.

devanagari: देवदुन्दुभयो नेदुर्गन्धर्वाप्सरसो जगुः । ववृषुः पुष्पवर्षाणि विबुधाः साधुवादिनः ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: devadundubhayo nedurgandharvāpsaraso jaguḥvavṛṣuḥ puṣpavarṣāṇi vibudhāḥ sādhuvādinaḥ
translation: (But it was also a joyous occasion as King Parīkṣit was absorbed in the Brahman). Drums were sounded in the heavens; Gandharvas and Apsaras (celestial musicians and dancers) sang. Gods, expressing their approbation, sent down showers of flowers.

devanagari: जनमेजयः स्वपितरं श्रुत्वा तक्षकभक्षितम् । यथाजुहाव सन्क्रुद्धो नागान्सत्रे सह द्विजैः ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: janamejayaḥ svapitaraṃ śrutvā takṣakabhakṣitamyathājuhāva sankruddho nāgānsatre saha dvijaiḥ
translation: Hearing that his father had been assassinated by Takṣaka, Janmejaya flared up with wrath. With the help of Brāhmaṇa priests, he started a sacrificial session in which he offered Nāgas as oblations into the fire, in the prescribed manner.

devanagari: सर्पसत्रे समिद्धाग्नौ दह्यमानान्महोरगान् । दृष्ट्वेन्द्रं भयसंविग्नस्तक्षकः शरणं ययौ ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: sarpasatre samiddhāgnau dahyamānānmahoragāndṛṣṭvendraṃ bhayasaṃvignastakṣakaḥ śaraṇaṃ yayau
translation: Seeing that even big serpents were being burnt in the raging fire-blaze of the serpent-sacrifice, Takṣaka became terror-stricken and sought the protection of Indra.

devanagari: अपश्यंस्तक्षकं तत्र राजा पारीक्षितो द्विजान् । उवाच तक्षकः कस्मान्न दह्येतोरगाधमः ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: apaśyaṃstakṣakaṃ tatra rājā pārīkṣito dvijānuvāca takṣakaḥ kasmānna dahyetoragādhamaḥ
translation: Not seeing Takṣaka (invoked and oblated) in the sacred fire kindled for the sacrifice, the king Janmejaya, the son of Parīkṣit, enquired why Takṣaka, the vilest of serpents, was not invoked as oblation to the fire.

devanagari: तं गोपायति राजेन्द्र शक्रः शरणमागतम् । तेन संस्तम्भितः सर्पस्तस्मान्नाग्नौ पतत्यसौ ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ gopāyati rājendra śakraḥ śaraṇamāgatamtena saṃstambhitaḥ sarpastasmānnāgnau patatyasau
translation: “O King of kings! It is Indra who is protecting Takṣaka as he has sought asylum with him. He has been protected and held back by Indra. Hence the serpent does not fall into the sacrificial fire.”

devanagari: पारीक्षित इति श्रुत्वा प्राहर्त्विज उदारधीः । सहेन्द्रस्तक्षको विप्रा नाग्नौ किमिति पात्यते ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: pārīkṣita iti śrutvā prāhartvija udāradhīḥsahendrastakṣako viprā nāgnau kimiti pātyate
translation: Hearing that explanation, the highly intelligent son of Parīkṣit asked the officiating priests, “Why is not Takṣaka along with Indra invoked and made to fall into the fire (by you) O Brāhmaṇas?”

devanagari: तच्छ्रुत्वाजुहुवुर्विप्राः सहेन्द्रं तक्षकं मखे । तक्षकाशु पतस्वेह सहेन्द्रेण मरुत्वता ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: tacchrutvājuhuvurviprāḥ sahendraṃ takṣakaṃ makhetakṣakāśu patasveha sahendreṇa marutvatā
translation: Hearing that request of Janmejaya) the Brāhmaṇas invoked Takṣaka along with Indra in that sacrifice thus: “O Takṣaka! Instantly fall into this (sacrificial) fire along with. Indra, the leader of Maruts.[5]
purport:
Footnote [5]:
Who is accompanied by Marut (wind)—gods—Bhāgavata Candrikā Padaratnāvalī

devanagari: इति ब्रह्मोदिताक्षेपैः स्थानादिन्द्रः प्रचालितः । बभूव सम्भ्रान्तमतिः सविमानः सतक्षकः ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: iti brahmoditākṣepaiḥ sthānādindraḥ pracālitaḥbabhūva sambhrāntamatiḥ savimānaḥ satakṣakaḥ
translation: In this way, due to the (superior) force in the harsh invocation of the Brāhmaṇas, Indra, along with his aerial car and Takṣaka, was dislodged from his position and became bewildered (in mind).

devanagari: तं पतन्तं विमानेन सहतक्षकमम्बरात् । विलोक्याङ्गिरसः प्राह राजानं तं बृहस्पतिः ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ patantaṃ vimānena sahatakṣakamambarātvilokyāṅgirasaḥ prāha rājānaṃ taṃ bṛhaspatiḥ
translation: Observing that Indra, along with his aerial car and Takṣaka, was crashing down from the sky, Bṛhaspati of the Aṅgiras gotra (clan) (strongly protested and) remonstrated with the king.[6]
purport:
Footnote [6]:
As per Mahābhārata, Ādi. chs. 56-58 it is Āstīka, the Yāyāvara who made Janmejaya to stop the sacrifice.

devanagari: नैष त्वया मनुष्येन्द्र वधमर्हति सर्पराट् । अनेन पीतममृतमथ वा अजरामरः ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: naiṣa tvayā manuṣyendra vadhamarhati sarparāṭanena pītamamṛtamatha vā ajarāmaraḥ
translation: “O King of men! This king of serpents cannot be killed by you, as nectar has been imbibed by him and he is hence immune from old age and death.

devanagari: जीवितं मरणं जन्तोर्गतिः स्वेनैव कर्मणा । राजंस्ततोऽन्यो नास्त्यस्य प्रदाता सुखदुःखयोः ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: jīvitaṃ maraṇaṃ jantorgatiḥ svenaiva karmaṇārājaṃstato'nyo nāstyasya pradātā sukhaduḥkhayoḥ
translation: (If you still insist on burning him as he is the murderer of your father please consider that Life and death of a being and his (consequent) position in the other world—all is determined by his own actions (Karma). Hence, O King, (it is the Karma—the result of one’s action and) no other person can be held responsible as the cause of happiness or misery of any other person.

devanagari: सर्पचौराग्निविद्युद्भ्यः क्षुत्तृड् । व्याध्यादिभिर्नृप । पञ्चत्वमृच्छते जन्तुर्भुङ्क्त आरब्धकर्म तत् ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: sarpacaurāgnividyudbhyaḥ kṣuttṛḍvyādhyādibhirnṛpapañcatvamṛcchate janturbhuṅkta ārabdhakarma tat
translation: When a being meets death through, the agency of a serpent, a thief, fire, lightning, starvation, thirst, disease and such other causes, the victim is experiencing the fruit of his own Karma that is in the process of fruition.

devanagari: तस्मात्सत्रमिदं राजन्संस्थीयेताभिचारिकम् । सर्पा अनागसो दग्धा जनैर्दिष्टं हि भुज्यते ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: tasmātsatramidaṃ rājansaṃsthīyetābhicārikamsarpā anāgaso dagdhā janairdiṣṭaṃ hi bhujyate
translation: Therefore, let this malevolently motivated sacrificial session involving violence to life be stopped, as serpents, in spite of their innocence, have been burnt O King! (I however do not blame you for what you have done so far) It is the fruit of one’s part action that is experienced by people.

devanagari: सूत उवाच । इत्युक्तः स तथेत्याह महर्षेर्मानयन्वचः । सर्पसत्रादुपरतः पूजयामास वाक्पतिम् ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: sūta uvācaityuktaḥ sa tathetyāha maharṣermānayanvacaḥsarpasatrāduparataḥ pūjayāmāsa vākpatim
translation:
Sūta said:
Thus appealed, the king respected the words of the great sage and said, “Let it be done so.” He desisted from that serpent sacrifice and worshipped Bṛhaspati.

devanagari: सैषा विष्णोर्महामाया बाध्ययालक्षणा यया । मुह्यन्त्यस्यैवात्मभूता भूतेषु गुणवृत्तिभिः ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: saiṣā viṣṇormahāmāyā bādhyayālakṣaṇā yayāmuhyantyasyaivātmabhūtā bhūteṣu guṇavṛttibhiḥ
translation: Such is this notable great deluding potency (Māyā) of Lord Viṣṇu. It is both incomprehensible and insuperable. It is due to the deluding force of this Māyā that beings, though rays or aṃśas of Viṣṇu succumb to passions (such as anger, etc.) which are the products of guṇas (like rajas and tamas) and do wrong to other fellow-beings.

devanagari: न यत्र दम्भीत्यभया विराजिता मायात्मवादेऽसकृदात्मवादिभिः । न यद्विवादो विविधस्तदाश्रयो मनश्च सङ्कल्पविकल्पवृत्ति यत् ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: na yatra dambhītyabhayā virājitā māyātmavāde'sakṛdātmavādibhiḥna yadvivādo vividhastadāśrayo manaśca saṅkalpavikalpavṛtti yat
translation: [7] (Māyā is superable if you resort to Lord Viṣṇu). The notion such as ‘This man is a hypocrite’ which exists in the mind is Māyā. But it does not figure boldly or openly in the frequent discussions held by the exponents of the self in their investigations about the self. (It is shown to exist somehow like one in a scared state of mind and does not function as a deluding potency). With relation to God there is no scope for various discussions or disputations which are based on Māyā as he is above that. Nor can the mind which is of the nature of creating ideations and doubts function in respect of God.
purport:
Footnote [7]:
Padaratnāvalī is more lucid:—“When persons who, being experts in investigating into Upaniṣads, repeatedly carry on their enquiry or discussion about Paramātman, Māyā which has the capacity of deluding beings, does not wield any influence over Hari, though it may remain incomprehensible to others. Brahman cannot be comprehended even by the mind which is ‘unstable’ due to its apprehensions and doubts. Here the deities presiding over the mind or speech are implied and not the physical organ. Even these deities cannot comprehend paramātman.”

devanagari: न यत्र सृज्यं सृजतोभयोः परं श्रेयश्च जीवस्त्रिभिरन्वितस्त्वहम् । तदेतदुत्सादितबाध्यबाधकं निषिध्य चोर्मीन्विरमेत तन्मुनिः ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: na yatra sṛjyaṃ sṛjatobhayoḥ paraṃ śreyaśca jīvastribhiranvitastvahamtadetadutsāditabādhyabādhakaṃ niṣidhya cormīnvirameta tanmuniḥ
translation: In relation to Him, no scope is there for the Jīva with its adjunct ahaṃkāra as its component and with Karma (action), materials bringing about Karma or its instruments and the fruit to be attained through the above two. He is above and unrelated to opposition or interruption (and hence does not oppose anybody or can be opposed by anybody). That being the nature of the self, the contemplative sage should discard these waves i.e. aberrations of ahaṃkāra and remain in the blissful state of ātman.

devanagari: परं पदं वैष्णवमामनन्ति तद्यन्नेति नेतीत्यतदुत्सिसृक्षवः । विसृज्य दौरात्म्यमनन्यसौहृदा हृदोपगुह्यावसितं समाहितैः ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: paraṃ padaṃ vaiṣṇavamāmananti tadyanneti netītyatadutsisṛkṣavaḥvisṛjya daurātmyamananyasauhṛdā hṛdopaguhyāvasitaṃ samāhitaiḥ
translation: Persons who after due examination (of the true nature of things) desire to renounce everything that is not Ātman (using the words ‘not that’, ‘not that’) declare that the essential supreme nature ofViṣṇu is as (described) above. They have given up the false notion of (the identification of the body and soul called here as) ‘I-ness’ and ‘mine-ness’ with reference to their body and belongings. With full control over their body, senses and mind they embrace the essential nature of Viṣṇu in their heart and realize the Ātman by their power of meditation.

devanagari: त एतदधिगच्छन्ति विष्णोर्यत्परमं पदम् । अहं ममेति दौर्जन्यं न येषां देहगेहजम् ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: ta etadadhigacchanti viṣṇoryatparamaṃ padamahaṃ mameti daurjanyaṃ na yeṣāṃ dehagehajam
translation: Only they can attain to the supreme nature of Viṣṇu, who are not touched by the evil notion of ‘I’ and ‘mine’ with reference to their body and house.

devanagari: अतिवादांस्तितिक्षेत नावमन्येत कञ्चन । न चेमं देहमाश्रित्य वैरं कुर्वीत केनचित् ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: ativādāṃstitikṣeta nāvamanyeta kañcanana cemaṃ dehamāśritya vairaṃ kurvīta kenacit
translation: One should tolerate disparaging remarks and harsh words; he should not show disrespect to anybody; nor should he cherish enmity to any one for the sake of this (physical) body (which is to be cast off one day).

devanagari: नमो भगवते तस्मै कृष्णायाकुण्ठमेधसे । यत्पादाम्बुरुहध्यानात्संहितामध्यगामिमाम् ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: namo bhagavate tasmai kṛṣṇāyākuṇṭhamedhaseyatpādāmburuhadhyānātsaṃhitāmadhyagāmimām
translation:
I bow to the worshipful and glorious Kṛṣṇa-Dvaipāyana Vyāsa[8] whose intelligence easily grasps everything (lit. is unobstructible). It is by meditating upon his feet that I learnt this compilation of Śrīmad Bhāgavata.
[See next for the second part of this chapter (i.e., verses 36-80)]
[back to top]

devanagari: श्रीशौनक उवाच । पैलादिभिर्व्यासशिष्यैर्वेदाचार्यैर्महात्मभिः । वेदाश्च कथिता व्यस्ता एतत्सौम्याभिधेहि नः ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīśaunaka uvācapailādibhirvyāsaśiṣyairvedācāryairmahātmabhiḥvedāśca kathitā vyastā etatsaumyābhidhehi naḥ
translation:
Śaunaka said:
Be pleased to tell us into how many branches (or schools) were the Vedas divided by Paila and others (viz. Vaiśampāyana, Jaimini, Sumantu and Śuka) great-souled teachers of the Vedas, the disciples of Vyāsa!

devanagari: सूत उवाच । समाहितात्मनो ब्रह्मन्ब्रह्मणः परमेष्ठिनः । हृद्याकाशादभून्नादो वृत्तिरोधाद्विभाव्यते ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: sūta uvācasamāhitātmano brahmanbrahmaṇaḥ parameṣṭhinaḥhṛdyākāśādabhūnnādo vṛttirodhādvibhāvyate
translation:
Śūta [Sūta?] said:
While god Brahmā, the occupant of the highest position in the creation, was meditating with well-composed mind, from the space (the sky-like, vacuum) in his heart arose a sound. The same sound is even perceptible when the function of the sense of hearing is closed.

devanagari: यदुपासनया ब्रह्मन्योगिनो मलमात्मनः । द्रव्यक्रियाकारकाख्यं धूत्वा यान्त्यपुनर्भवम् ॥ ३८ ॥
english_devnagari: yadupāsanayā brahmanyogino malamātmanaḥdravyakriyākārakākhyaṃ dhūtvā yāntyapunarbhavam
translation: By concentrating on that sound (nāda)[1] O sage, yogins wipe out all the impurities of the mind caused by the Adhibhūta, the Adhyātma and the Adhidaiva[2] and attain, to Liberation from saṃsāra (lit. the state from which there is no return).
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Concentration on the Nāda (sound) is a special Type of Yoga.

Footnote [2]:
These terms arc variously interpreted: Thus dravya or adhi-bhūta is taken as pertaining to the bhūtas or elements the body; Krīyā or adḥyātma as organs of actions (Padaratnāvalī) but karma—merit as well as sin (Bhāgavata Candrikā); Kāraka or adhidaiva as sense of perception’ (Padaratnāvalī) Sense-organs (Bhāgavata Candrikā).

devanagari: ततोऽभूत्त्रिवृदॐकारो योऽव्यक्तप्रभवः स्वराट् । यत्तल्लिङ्गं भगवतो ब्रह्मणः परमात्मनः ॥ ३९ ॥
english_devnagari: tato'bhūttrivṛdaoṃkāro yo'vyaktaprabhavaḥ svarāṭyattalliṅgaṃ bhagavato brahmaṇaḥ paramātmanaḥ
translation: From that nāda (sound) arose the sacred syllable Om which consists of three mātrās (viz. a, u, m,) the source of which is unmanifest[3] and shines (i.e. becomes manifest in the heart) by itself. That Om is the special index of the glorious Brahman, the Supreme Soul.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
aprabhava (i) The source of which is too subtle to be understood by those who are not adept in Yoga (Bhāgavata Candrikā).
(ii) Whose source is the unmanifest Hari (Padaratnāvalī).

devanagari: शृणोति य इमं स्फोटं सुप्तश्रोत्रे च शून्यदृक् । येन वाग्व्यज्यते यस्य व्यक्तिराकाश आत्मनः ॥ ४० ॥
english_devnagari: śṛṇoti ya imaṃ sphoṭaṃ suptaśrotre ca śūnyadṛkyena vāgvyajyate yasya vyaktirākāśa ātmanaḥ
translation: He (god Brahmā) hears the unmanifest syllable OM even when the auditory sense has ceased to work (as when the ears are closed) and whose cognition remains unaffected even when all senses become function-less[4], (as in Samādhi or in deep sleep). It is that sacred syllable OM which, evolving from Paramātman, becomes manifest (audible) in the cavity of the heart and produces the great speech called the Vedas.
purport:
Footnote [4]:
This is not possible in the case of Jīva who depends on sense-organs (Bhāvāratha Dīpikā).

devanagari: स्वधाम्नो ब्राह्मणः साक्षाद्वाचकः परमात्मनः । स सर्वमन्त्रोपनिषद्वेदबीजं सनातनम् ॥ ४१ ॥
english_devnagari: svadhāmno brāhmaṇaḥ sākṣādvācakaḥ paramātmanaḥsa sarvamantropaniṣadvedabījaṃ sanātanam
translation: [5] It (Praṇava or OM) directly and comprehensively expresses the Supreme Soul (Paramātman), the Brahman itself which is its source. It is the eternal seed of all the Mantras, Vedas and Upaniṣads.
purport:
Footnote [5]:
Both Bhāgavata Candrikā and Padaratnāvalī use the adjectives in this verse as qualifying the Brahman e.g. the Brahman who is the most powerful in its own essential glory. Padaratnāvalī states that previously OM is called ‘a significant index (liṅga) of the Brahman, here it is described as its comprehensive (vācaka) expression and the seed of Mantras and Vedas which when pronounced and articulated properly have the potency to give the fruit of all Mantras:
acchinnatvenoccāryamāṇaṃ sarva-mantrādīnāṃ japtānām phala-dāna-sāmarthyaṃ janavati /

devanagari: तस्य ह्यासंस्त्रयो वर्णा अकाराद्या भृगूद्वह । धार्यन्ते यैस्त्रयो भावा गुणनामार्थवृत्तयः ॥ ४२ ॥
english_devnagari: tasya hyāsaṃstrayo varṇā akārādyā bhṛgūdvahadhāryante yaistrayo bhāvā guṇanāmārthavṛttayaḥ
translation: It i.e. OM is constituted of three sounds: A and others viz. U and M, O most prominent member of the Bhṛgu clan. These sounds—A, U and M severally contain (the seed of) the triads of entities, viz. guṇas (modes of prakṛti, viz. sattva, rajas and tamas), names (the Vedas—Ṛg, Sāman and Yayus), substances[6] (the three spheres or worlds known as bhūḥ—the earth, bhuvaḥ—the sky and the aerial region svaḥ—the heaven and states of consciousness (wakefulness, dream and deep sleep).
purport:
Footnote [6]:
arthaḥ: Bhāgavata Candrikā, as a Viśiṣṭādvaitin, proposes the following triad:
(i) Jīvātman deserves to be a part and parcel of Paramātman. (ii) He depends for shelter on Paramātman and is (iii) fit to be enjoyed by him.

devanagari: ततोऽक्षरसमाम्नायमसृजद्भगवानजः । अन्तस्थोष्मस्वरस्पर्श ह्रस्वदीर्घादिलक्षणम् ॥ ४३ ॥
english_devnagari: tato'kṣarasamāmnāyamasṛjadbhagavānajaḥantasthoṣmasvarasparśa hrasvadīrghādilakṣaṇam
translation: Out of the (above) three syllables, the glorious Lotus-born god Brahmā evolved the alphabets consisting of semi-vowels (y, v, r, l,) aspirants (ś, ṣ, s, h), vowels, consonants (viz. gutturals, palatals, cerebrals, dentals, and labials) short, long and predated accents or sound measures.)

devanagari: तेनासौ चतुरो वेदांश्चतुर्भिर्वदनैर्विभुः । सव्याहृतिकान्सॐकारांश्चातुर्होत्रविवक्षया ॥ ४४ ॥
english_devnagari: tenāsau caturo vedāṃścaturbhirvadanairvibhuḥsavyāhṛtikānsaoṃkārāṃścāturhotravivakṣayā
translation: With the help of these sounds (letters of the alphabet) Brahmā, through his four mouths, gave expression to four Vedas together with the vyāhṛtis (bhūḥ, bhuvaḥ, svaḥ) and the sacred syllable OM. He intended to lay down the duties of the priests, viz., hotā, adhvaryu, udgātā and Brahmā officiating at sacrifice.

devanagari: पुत्रानध्यापयत्तांस्तु ब्रह्मर्षीन्ब्रह्मकोविदान् । ते तु धर्मोपदेष्टारः स्वपुत्रेभ्यः समादिशन् ॥ ४५ ॥
english_devnagari: putrānadhyāpayattāṃstu brahmarṣīnbrahmakovidānte tu dharmopadeṣṭāraḥ svaputrebhyaḥ samādiśan
translation: He taught the Vedas to his sons (like Marīci and others) who were Brāhmaṇa sages and made them experts in the Vedic pronunciation and intonation. They became the promulgators of dharma and taught the same, in their turn, to their sons.

devanagari: ते परम्परया प्राप्तास्तत्तच्छिष्यैर्धृतव्रतैः । चतुर्युगेष्वथ व्यस्ता द्वापरादौ महर्षिभिः ॥ ४६ ॥
english_devnagari: te paramparayā prāptāstattacchiṣyairdhṛtavrataiḥcaturyugeṣvatha vyastā dvāparādau maharṣibhiḥ
translation: During the course of the four Yugas those Vedas were handed clown from generation to generation by their disciples who were strict in the observance of Vows. These Vedas were rearranged by great sages at the beginning of the Dvāpara age[7].
purport:
Footnote [7]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā corrects this statement by pointing out that it was at the end of the Dvāpara age, at the time of King Śantanu, that Vyāsa re-arranged the Vedas.

devanagari: क्षीणायुषः क्षीणसत्त्वान्दुर्मेधान्वीक्ष्य कालतः । वेदान्ब्रह्मर्षयो व्यस्यन्हृदिस्थाच्युतचोदिताः ॥ ४७ ॥
english_devnagari: kṣīṇāyuṣaḥ kṣīṇasattvāndurmedhānvīkṣya kālataḥvedānbrahmarṣayo vyasyanhṛdisthācyutacoditāḥ
translation: Observing that men are having decreasing span of life growing less and less energetic and becoming poorer in intellectual capacity, the Brāhmaṇa sages, as directed by Lord Viṣṇu, enthroned in their heart, re-arranged the Vedas.

devanagari: अस्मिन्नप्यन्तरे ब्रह्मन्भगवान्लोकभावनः । ब्रह्मेशाद्यैर्लोकपालैर्याचितो धर्मगुप्तये ॥ ४८ ॥
english_devnagari: asminnapyantare brahmanbhagavānlokabhāvanaḥbrahmeśādyairlokapālairyācito dharmaguptaye
translation: Even in this (Vaivasvata Manvantara also, O sage, the glorious Lord, the Protector of the world was prayed by the rulers of various spheres like god Brahmā, Ṛuḍra and others to protect the path of righteousness.

devanagari: पराशरात्सत्यवत्यामंशांशकलया विभुः । अवतीर्णो महाभाग वेदं चक्रे चतुर्विधम् ॥ ४९ ॥
english_devnagari: parāśarātsatyavatyāmaṃśāṃśakalayā vibhuḥavatīrṇo mahābhāga vedaṃ cakre caturvidham
translation: (Responding favourably to their prayers) the Almighty Lord descended as the son of Parāśara and Satyavatī, out of a ray (particle) of Sattva which is but a part of the Māyā. He divided the Veda into four parts, O fortunate sage!

devanagari: ऋगथर्वयजुःसाम्नां राशीरुद्धृत्य वर्गशः । चतस्रः संहिताश्चक्रे मन्त्रैर्मणिगणा इव ॥ ५० ॥
english_devnagari: ṛgatharvayajuḥsāmnāṃ rāśīruddhṛtya vargaśaḥcatasraḥ saṃhitāścakre mantrairmaṇigaṇā iva
translation: Just as (out of a heap of mixed up precious stones) precious stones of the same kind are assorted and kept in separate heaps, he (Vyāsa) selected and classified the Vedic Mantras as belonging to the category of the Ṛg, Atharvan, Yajus, and Sāman and thus compiled four Saṃhitās or collections out of those (formerly mixed up) Mantras.

devanagari: तासां स चतुरः शिष्यानुपाहूय महामतिः । एकैकां संहितां ब्रह्मन्नेकैकस्मै ददौ विभुः ॥ ५१ ॥
english_devnagari: tāsāṃ sa caturaḥ śiṣyānupāhūya mahāmatiḥekaikāṃ saṃhitāṃ brahmannekaikasmai dadau vibhuḥ
translation: The vastly intelligent Vyāsa summoned four of his pupils and entrusted to each of them one of the Saṃhitās, O Śaunaka.

devanagari: पैलाय संहितामाद्यां बह्वृचाख्यां उवाच ह । वैशम्पायनसंज्ञाय निगदाख्यं यजुर्गणम् ॥ ५२ ॥
english_devnagari: pailāya saṃhitāmādyāṃ bahvṛcākhyāṃ uvāca havaiśampāyanasaṃjñāya nigadākhyaṃ yajurgaṇam
translation: As the tradition goes, he taught the first compilation called Bahvṛca[8] or the Ṛgveda to Paila and to Vaiśampāyana, the compilation called Yajurveda which (being a collection of prose texts to be recited at the sacrifice) is also called Nigada.
purport:
Footnote [8]:
Bahvṛc so called as it consists of the biggest number of Mantras.

devanagari: साम्नां जैमिनये प्राह तथा छन्दोगसंहिताम् । अथर्वाङ्गिरसीं नाम स्वशिष्याय सुमन्तवे ॥ ५३ ॥
english_devnagari: sāmnāṃ jaiminaye prāha tathā chandogasaṃhitāmatharvāṅgirasīṃ nāma svaśiṣyāya sumantave
translation: He taught the compilation of Sāma-Veda or the Chandoga saṃhitā (compilation of mantras to be sung according to metre in which they are composed) to Jaimini. And the compilation of Atharvāṅgiraṣī or the Atharva Veda to his disciple named Sumantu.

devanagari: पैलः स्वसंहितामूचे इन्द्र प्रमितये मुनिः । बाष्कलाय च सोऽप्याह शिष्येभ्यः संहितां स्वकाम् ॥ ५४ ॥
english_devnagari: pailaḥ svasaṃhitāmūce indra pramitaye muniḥbāṣkalāya ca so'pyāha śiṣyebhyaḥ saṃhitāṃ svakām
translation: (Dividing the Ṛgveda into two branches) the sage Paila taught that Saṃhitā to Indra-pramiti and Bāṣkala. Bāṣkala on his part divided the Saṃhitā taught to him in four branches and taught one each respectively to (his pupils) Bodhya, Yājñavalkya, Parāśara and Agnimitra, O descendant of Bhṛgu (i.e. Śaunaka). Indrapramiti, a man of controlled mind, taught his Saṃhitā to the learned sage Māṇḍūkeya (who was his son). Māṇḍūkeya’s disciple was Devamitra who taught it to Saubhari and others.

devanagari: चतुर्धा व्यस्य बोध्याय याज्ञवल्क्याय भार्गव । पराशरायाग्निमित्र इन्द्र प्रमितिरात्मवान् ॥ ५५ ॥
english_devnagari: caturdhā vyasya bodhyāya yājñavalkyāya bhārgavaparāśarāyāgnimitra indra pramitirātmavān
translation: (Dividing the Ṛgveda into two branches) the sage Paila taught that Saṃhitā to Indra-pramiti and Bāṣkala. Bāṣkala on his part divided the Saṃhitā taught to him in four branches and taught one each respectively to (his pupils) Bodhya, Yājñavalkya, Parāśara and Agnimitra, O descendant of Bhṛgu (i.e. Śaunaka). Indrapramiti, a man of controlled mind, taught his Saṃhitā to the learned sage Māṇḍūkeya (who was his son). Māṇḍūkeya’s disciple was Devamitra who taught it to Saubhari and others.

devanagari: अध्यापयत्संहितां स्वां माण्डूकेयमृषिं कविम् । तस्य शिष्यो देवमित्रः सौभर्यादिभ्य ऊचिवान् ॥ ५६ ॥
english_devnagari: adhyāpayatsaṃhitāṃ svāṃ māṇḍūkeyamṛṣiṃ kavimtasya śiṣyo devamitraḥ saubharyādibhya ūcivān
translation: (Dividing the Ṛgveda into two branches) the sage Paila taught that Saṃhitā to Indra-pramiti and Bāṣkala. Bāṣkala on his part divided the Saṃhitā taught to him in four branches and taught one each respectively to (his pupils) Bodhya, Yājñavalkya, Parāśara and Agnimitra, O descendant of Bhṛgu (i.e. Śaunaka). Indrapramiti, a man of controlled mind, taught his Saṃhitā to the learned sage Māṇḍūkeya (who was his son). Māṇḍūkeya’s disciple was Devamitra who taught it to Saubhari and others.

devanagari: शाकल्यस्तत्सुतः स्वां तु पञ्चधा व्यस्य संहिताम् । वात्स्यमुद्गलशालीय गोखल्यशिशिरेष्वधात् ॥ ५७ ॥
english_devnagari: śākalyastatsutaḥ svāṃ tu pañcadhā vyasya saṃhitāmvātsyamudgalaśālīya gokhalyaśiśireṣvadhāt
translation: Śākalya was Māṇḍukeya’s son. He divided his Saṃhitā into five branches and imparted the knowledge of one each to his pupils, Vātsya, Mudgala, Śālīya, Gokhalya, and Śiśira.

devanagari: जातूकर्ण्यश्च तच्छिष्यः सनिरुक्तां स्वसंहिताम् । बलाकपैलजाबाल विरजेभ्यो ददौ मुनिः ॥ ५८ ॥
english_devnagari: jātūkarṇyaśca tacchiṣyaḥ saniruktāṃ svasaṃhitāmbalākapailajābāla virajebhyo dadau muniḥ
translation: The sage Jātūkarṇya was another disciple of Śākalya. He (divided his Sarṅhitā into three parts) taught his Saṃhitā together with its Nirukta (etymological explanation of Vedic words) to his (four) pupils, Balāka, Paija, Vaitāla and Viraja.

devanagari: बाष्कलिः प्रतिशाखाभ्यो वालखिल्याख्यसंहिताम् । चक्रे बालायनिर्भज्यः कासारश्चैव तां दधुः ॥ ५९ ॥
english_devnagari: bāṣkaliḥ pratiśākhābhyo vālakhilyākhyasaṃhitāmcakre bālāyanirbhajyaḥ kāsāraścaiva tāṃ dadhuḥ
translation: Bāṣkala’s son, Bāṣkali, carved out of all the above mentioned branches a new Saṃhitā called Vālakhilya Saṃhitā which was mastered and preserved by (his disciples) Bālāyani, Bhajya and Kāsāra.

devanagari: बह्वृचाः संहिता ह्येता एभिर्ब्रह्मर्षिभिर्धृताः । श्रुत्वैतच्छन्दसां व्यासं सर्वपापैः प्रमुच्यते ॥ ६० ॥
english_devnagari: bahvṛcāḥ saṃhitā hyetā ebhirbrahmarṣibhirdhṛtāḥśrutvaitacchandasāṃ vyāsaṃ sarvapāpaiḥ pramucyate
translation: These compilations (branches) of the Bahvṛca or the Ṛgveda were learnt and preserved by these Brāhmaṇa sages. He who listens to the classification of the Vedas is absolved of all sins. (Now follows the account of the Taittirīya Saṃhitā of the Yajurveda).

devanagari: वैशम्पायनशिष्या वै चरकाध्वर्यवोऽभवन् । यच्चेरुर्ब्रह्महत्यांहः क्षपणं स्वगुरोर्व्रतम् ॥ ६१ ॥
english_devnagari: vaiśampāyanaśiṣyā vai carakādhvaryavo'bhavanyaccerurbrahmahatyāṃhaḥ kṣapaṇaṃ svagurorvratam
translation: It is traditionally reported that Vaiśampāyana had pupils called Carakādhvaryus.[9] For the sake of their preceptor, they performed a penance in expiation of the sin of Brahma- hatyā (killing a Brāhmaṇa).
purport:
Footnote [9]:
The term is variously explained: Pupils called
(i) Carakas and Adhvaryus;
(ii) Carakas who became Adhvaryus (experts in the Yajurveda)
Caraka—(1) One who observed a vow (2) Pilgrims to places of holy water (Padaratnāvalī)
Adhvaryus—experts in conducting sacrifices. (Padaratnāvalī).

devanagari: याज्ञवल्क्यश्च तच्छिष्य आहाहो भगवन्कियत् । चरितेनाल्पसाराणां चरिष्येऽहं सुदुश्चरम् ॥ ६२ ॥
english_devnagari: yājñavalkyaśca tacchiṣya āhāho bhagavankiyatcaritenālpasārāṇāṃ cariṣye'haṃ suduścaram
translation: One of his disciples, Yājñavalkya, remarked (to Vaiśampāyana). “O worshipful Sir, what (insignificant) amount of reward (in the form of expiation of the sin) can be gained through the poor performance of penance by these weaklings? I alone can undergo such austere penance as will be extremely difficult for others to perform (and will alone absolve you of the sin of Brahma-hatyā).”

devanagari: इत्युक्तो गुरुरप्याह कुपितो याह्यलं त्वया । विप्रावमन्त्रा शिष्येण मदधीतं त्यजाश्विति ॥ ६३ ॥
english_devnagari: ityukto gururapyāha kupito yāhyalaṃ tvayāviprāvamantrā śiṣyeṇa madadhītaṃ tyajāśviti
translation: When (boastfully) addressed thus, the preceptor (Vaiśampāyana) got offended. He (peremptorily) ordered; “Get out. Enough of a pupil like you who contemptuously speak of Brāhmaṇa sages. Give up at once whatever you have learnt from me.”

devanagari: देवरातसुतः सोऽपि छर्दित्वा यजुषां गणम् । ततो गतोऽथ मुनयो ददृशुस्तान्यजुर्गणान् ॥ ६४ ॥
english_devnagari: devarātasutaḥ so'pi charditvā yajuṣāṃ gaṇamtato gato'tha munayo dadṛśustānyajurgaṇān
translation: Thereupon, the son of Devarāta (Yājñavalkya) on his part immediately vomitted out the collection of Yajurveda passages (that he learnt from his preceptor) and left the place. And the sages saw the collection of the Yajurveda texts.

devanagari: यजूंषि तित्तिरा भूत्वा तल्लोलुपतयाऽददुः । तैत्तिरीया इति यजुः शाखा आसन्सुपेशलाः ॥ ६५ ॥
english_devnagari: yajūṃṣi tittirā bhūtvā tallolupatayā'daduḥtaittirīyā iti yajuḥ śākhā āsansupeśalāḥ
translation: (At that sight) the sages were so enamoured of it (and greedily wanted to master as much of it as they could. But acceptance of vomition being disgraceful to Brāhmaṇas) they assumed the form of Tittira birds (francoline Partridges) and collected it. Hence the beautiful branches of the Yajurveda came to be known as Taittirīyas. (The origin of the branches called Kāṇva, Mādhyandina of the white Yajurveda is now explained).

devanagari: याज्ञवल्क्यस्ततो ब्रह्मंश्छन्दांस्यधि गवेषयन् । गुरोरविद्यमानानि सूपतस्थेऽर्कमीश्वरम् ॥ ६६ ॥
english_devnagari: yājñavalkyastato brahmaṃśchandāṃsyadhi gaveṣayanguroravidyamānāni sūpatasthe'rkamīśvaram
translation: Thereupon, in search of Śruti portions not known even to his preceptor, Yājñavalkya devoutly worshipped and prayed the sun-god, the Master of Vedas[10] as follows:
purport:
Footnote [10]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā’s quotation:
ṛgbhiḥ pūrvāhṇe divi-deva īyate, yajurvede tiṣṭhati, madhye ahnaḥ
sāma-vedenāstamaye mahīyate
vedair aśūnyas tribhireti sūryaḥ / /

devanagari: श्रीयाज्ञवल्क्य उवाच । ॐ नमो भगवते आदित्यायाखिलजगतामात्मस्वरूपेण काल । स्वरूपेण चतुर्विधभूतनिकायानां ब्रह्मादिस्तम्बपर्यन्तानामन्तर्हृद...
english_devnagari: śrīyājñavalkya uvācaoṃ namo bhagavate ādityāyākhilajagatāmātmasvarūpeṇa kālasvarūpeṇa caturvidhabhūtanikāyānāṃ brahmādistambaparyantānāmantarhṛdayeṣubahirapi cākāśa ivopādhināvyavadhīyamāno bhavānekaeva kṣaṇalavanimeṣāvayavopacitasaṃvatsaragaṇenāpāmādānavisargābhyāmimāṃ lokayātrāmanuvahati
translation:
Yājñavalkya implored:
Salutations to the glorious sun-god denoted by the syllable OM[11]—The sun-god who, though one, abides in the form of ātman, in the whole of the universe, enthroned in the hearts of four categories of beings (viz. oviparous, viviparous, Sweat-born, sprouted from the earth i.e. the plants, etc.) beginning from god Brahmā down to a clump of grass but who (though inside like a Jīva) is not circumscribed by any condition, who like the limitless sky envelops the universe from the out-side as well in the form of Time. The almighty God maintains, all alone, the life and progress of the world by evaporating waters’ (during the summer) and releasing them back (as rain-showers during the rainy season) throughout years constituted of parts of time such as a moment, an instant, an eye- wink, etc. (This explains the Vareṇyatva stated in the first foot of the Gāyatrī-mantra).
purport:
Footnote [11]:
Padaratnāvalī: Salutations to the Supreme Lord denoted by the syllable Oṃ, the Brahman itself. You are endowed with six excellent attributes which show your supreme nature as applied to sages like Vasiṣṭha). You are eternal preceding all creation (ādityāya).

devanagari: यदु ह वाव विबुधर्षभ सवितरदस्तपत्यनुसवनमहर् । अहराम्नायविधिनोपतिष्ठमानानामखिलदुरितवृजिन । बीजावभर्जन भगवतः समभिधीमहि तपन मण्डलम् ॥ ६८ ॥
english_devnagari: yadu ha vāva vibudharṣabha savitaradastapatyanusavanamaharaharāmnāyavidhinopatiṣṭhamānānāmakhiladuritavṛjinabījāvabharjana bhagavataḥ samabhidhīmahi tapana maṇḍalam
translation: O Prominent god! The creator of the world! We devoutly and properly as per Vedic procedure) meditate upon that celebrated orb of yours which is shining over these. O god of blazing splendour! You burn down all the sins and (the resultant) miseries as well as their seed (ignorance) in the case of those who worship (and contemplate upon) you as per prescriptions laid down in the Vedas. We worship you thrice a day (in the morning, the mid-day and the evening) as per rules laid down in the Vedas. (This explains the second foot of the Gāyatrī Mantra).

devanagari: य इह वाव स्थिरचरनिकराणां निजनिकेतनानां मनैन्द्रियासु । गणाननात्मनः स्वयमात्मान्तर्यामी प्रचोदयति ॥ ६९ ॥
english_devnagari: ya iha vāva sthiracaranikarāṇāṃ nijaniketanānāṃ manaindriyāsugaṇānanātmanaḥ svayamātmāntaryāmī pracodayati
translation: You of your own accord become the soul, the inner controller of the multitudes of mobile and immobile beings in this world, which depend on you for their very existence and direct their minds, sense-organs, vital breaths which are distinct from the soul and hence inert matter. (This explains the third foot of the Gāyatrī).

devanagari: य एवेमं लोकमतिकरालवदनान्धकारसंज्ञाजगरग्रह । गिलितं मृतकमिव विचेतनमवलोक्यानुकम्पया परमकारुणिक । ईक्षयैवोत्थाप्याहरहरनुसवनं श्रेयसि स्वधर...
english_devnagari: ya evemaṃ lokamatikarālavadanāndhakārasaṃjñājagaragrahagilitaṃ mṛtakamiva vicetanamavalokyānukampayā paramakāruṇikaīkṣayaivotthāpyāharaharanusavanaṃ śreyasi svadharmākhyātmāvasthane pravartayati
translation: Beholding that this world is swallowed up by an extremely horrendous boaconstrictor (of terrible jaws) called ‘darkness’ and is bereft of consciousness as if it is dead, you, being extremely tender-hearted, resuscitated it by your compassionate glance and directed it to attend to the course of performing their religious duties thrice every day which ultimately lead to the summum bonum and you go about like the sovereign of the world instilling terror in the hearts of the wicked.

devanagari: अवनिपतिरिवासाधूनां भयमुदीरयन्नटति परित आशापालैस् । तत्र तत्र कमलकोशाञ्जलिभिरुपहृतार्हणः ॥ ७१ ॥
english_devnagari: avanipatirivāsādhūnāṃ bhayamudīrayannaṭati parita āśāpālaistatra tatra kamalakośāñjalibhirupahṛtārhaṇaḥ
translation: On all sides, at every stage, you are worshipped by the guardian deities of the cardinal points with presents in their lotus-bud-like joined palms.

devanagari: अथ ह भगवंस्तव चरणनलिनयुगलं त्रिभुवनगुरुभिरभिवन्दितम् । अहमयातयामयजुष्काम उपसरामीति ॥ ७२ ॥
english_devnagari: atha ha bhagavaṃstava caraṇanalinayugalaṃ tribhuvanagurubhirabhivanditamahamayātayāmayajuṣkāma upasarāmīti
translation: Hence, as I am desirous of learning those texts of the Yajurveda which are fresh (i.e. not properly known to others), I approach and worship your lotus-feet which are bowed down by (Indra and other) rulers of the three worlds.” Thus prayed Yajñavalkya.

devanagari: सूत उवाच । एवं स्तुतः स भगवान्वाजिरूपधरो रविः । यजूंष्ययातयामानि मुनयेऽदात्प्रसादितः ॥ ७३ ॥
english_devnagari: sūta uvācaevaṃ stutaḥ sa bhagavānvājirūpadharo raviḥyajūṃṣyayātayāmāni munaye'dātprasāditaḥ
translation:
Sūta said:
Being thus extolled and pleased thereby, the glorious Lord Hari i.e. the sun-god, assumed the form of a horse and out of grace, imparted to that sage the knowledge of those portions of the Yajuṛveda which were not stale (i.e. known to others).

devanagari: यजुर्भिरकरोच्छाखा दश पञ्च शतैर्विभुः । जगृहुर्वाजसन्यस्ताः काण्वमाध्यन्दिनादयः ॥ ७४ ॥
english_devnagari: yajurbhirakarocchākhā daśa pañca śatairvibhuḥjagṛhurvājasanyastāḥ kāṇvamādhyandinādayaḥ
translation:
That (spiritually) powerful sage classified hundreds of those mantras of the Yajurveda into fifteen branches, known as Vājasanīs (They were called Vājasanī as they were delivered very rapidly from the hair of his mane by the sun god in the form of a horse) Kāṇva, Mādhyandina and other sages learnt those branches.
(Now begins the topic of Sāma-veda)

devanagari: जैमिनेः समगस्यासीत्सुमन्तुस्तनयो मुनिः । सुत्वांस्तु तत्सुतस्ताभ्यामेकैकां प्राह संहिताम् ॥ ७५ ॥
english_devnagari: jaimineḥ samagasyāsītsumantustanayo muniḥsutvāṃstu tatsutastābhyāmekaikāṃ prāha saṃhitām
translation: Jaimini, the Chanter of the Sāma-Veda had a son called Sumantu who was (already) a sage. He had a son called Sunvan. To each of them, Jaimini taught a separate branch of the (Sāma-Veda) saṃhitā.

devanagari: सुकर्मा चापि तच्छिष्यः सामवेदतरोर्महान् । सहस्रसंहिताभेदं चक्रे साम्नां ततो द्विज ॥ ७६ ॥
english_devnagari: sukarmā cāpi tacchiṣyaḥ sāmavedatarormahānsahasrasaṃhitābhedaṃ cakre sāmnāṃ tato dvija
translation: Jaimini had another disciple called Sukarmā who was a Brāhmaṇa of vast intellectual capacity. He classified the compilation of Sāman hymns into one thousand branches (lit. he made the tree of the Sāmaveda grow into a thousand branches)

devanagari: हिरण्यनाभः कौशल्यः पौष्यञ्जिश्च सुकर्मणः । शिष्यौ जगृहतुश्चान्य आवन्त्यो ब्रह्मवित्तमः ॥ ७७ ॥
english_devnagari: hiraṇyanābhaḥ kauśalyaḥ pauṣyañjiśca sukarmaṇaḥśiṣyau jagṛhatuścānya āvantyo brahmavittamaḥ
translation: Hiraṇyanābha of Kosala (country) and Pauṣyañji—both disciples of Sukarman accepted (i.e. learnt) the entire Sāmaveda. So did another disciple Āvantya (a resident of Avanti) who was the most prominent knower of Brahma (Veda).

devanagari: उदीच्याः सामगाः शिष्या आसन्पञ्चशतानि वै । पौष्यञ्ज्यावन्त्ययोश्चापि तांश्च प्राच्यान्प्रचक्षते ॥ ७८ ॥
english_devnagari: udīcyāḥ sāmagāḥ śiṣyā āsanpañcaśatāni vaipauṣyañjyāvantyayoścāpi tāṃśca prācyānpracakṣate
translation: Pauṣyañji and Āvantya as well, each had five hundred disciples. They say that Pauṣyañji, Āvantya as well as Hiraṇyanābha[12] had five hundred disciples who learnt as many branches of Sāma, They hailed from the northern part of the country and hence arc called ‘Northerners’ though some call them “easterners”.
purport:
Footnote [12]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā quotes Viṣṇu-purāṇa 3.6.4. b and 5 to support this.

devanagari: लौगाक्षिर्माङ्गलिः कुल्यः कुशीदः कुक्षिरेव च । पौष्यञ्जिशिष्या जगृहुः संहितास्ते शतं शतम् ॥ ७९ ॥
english_devnagari: laugākṣirmāṅgaliḥ kulyaḥ kuśīdaḥ kukṣireva capauṣyañjiśiṣyā jagṛhuḥ saṃhitāste śataṃ śatam
translation: Laugākṣi, Māṅgali, Kulya, Kuśīda and Kukṣi—the (other) pupils of Pauṣyañji accepted (learnt) hundred compilations (Branches) of Sāmaveda each.

devanagari: कृतो हिरण्यनाभस्य चतुर्विंशति संहिताः । शिष्य ऊचे स्वशिष्येभ्यः शेषा आवन्त्य आत्मवान् ॥ ८० ॥
english_devnagari: kṛto hiraṇyanābhasya caturviṃśati saṃhitāḥśiṣya ūce svaśiṣyebhyaḥ śeṣā āvantya ātmavān
translation:
Kṛta, the disciple of Hiraṇyanābha taught twenty- four compilations (of Sāma hymns) to his pupils and Āvantya, a man of self-control, taught the remaining collections to his students.
[back to top]
